summary: The post argues that Silicon Valley startups are still built as assets to be sold, not businesses meant to generate and keep profit. Even as AI-era companies reach revenue faster with smaller teams, the incentive remains a big liquidity event, so “efficiency” is a temporary aesthetic rather than a durable business model.
money_quotes: In other words, despite the lofty language that people use to talk about Silicon Valley and its ambitions to build enduring companies, very few startups are, or even aspire to be, businesses that make money.
Instead, they are assets that are meant to be sold.
Efficiently making money wasn’t inherently good, because it was never the final goal.
Businesses care about profit; assets care about perception.
Compete with a factory of overwhelming compute at your own peril, y’all.
key_insight: AI-era efficiency changes startup mechanics, but not incentives—most startups still optimize for a one-time sale, not durable profits.
